summary: Gruber traces HTC's trajectory from peak profitability in 2011 to a 70% profit decline one year later, using the company's reversal on open bootloaders as a telling narrative thread. When HTC was riding high, CEO Peter Chou announced they would stop locking bootloaders in response to customer demand. Now, with profits cratering, HTC's flagship AT&T phone ships with a locked bootloader that can't be unlocked. Gruber implies that HTC's real customers were always the carriers, not end users, and financial pressure made that hierarchy explicit. He connects HTC's decline to the broader collapse of handset profits outside Apple and Samsung.
key_points: HTC went from 197% profit growth in Q1 2011 to a 70% profit decline in Q1 2012
HTC's 2011 promise to unlock bootloaders was quietly reversed under carrier pressure when profits fell
The 'customers' HTC listens to shifted from enthusiast users to carrier executives once financial reality set in
HTC's decline is part of a broader industry trend where nearly all handset makers except Apple are losing profit share
The difference is HTC was still rising as recently as a year ago, making the reversal especially sharp
money_quotes: I think it's safe to assume that the "customers" and "people" who wanted access to open bootloaders did not include the executives at major mobile carriers.
I'd say it seems pretty clear HTC is now listening to a different set of "customers".
The difference between HTC and companies like Nokia, RIM, LG, and Sony is that, as of a year ago, HTC was still seeing its share of the industry's profits rise.
The others have, since 2007, mostly seen their share of the industry's profits fall.
That's a staggering 70 percent decline compared to net profits the company booked in the same period last year.
key_insight: When an Android handset maker's profits collapse, the carrier relationship reasserts its dominance and user-friendly promises get quietly reversed.
